title: Expressing Opinions and Feelings
description: The functional language of opinion and emotion: give your view with creo que / me parece que, master the affirmative-indicative vs negated-subjunctive flip, react to things with value judgments and emotions (es lógico que, me molesta que), agree and disagree, and weigh certainty against doubt.

cards.front: Creo que / Pienso que / Me parece que
cards.back: I think / I believe / It seems to me that (+ indicative)
cards.notes: To state an opinion you assert as true, use **creo que**, **pienso que** or **me parece que** followed by the **indicative**: *Creo que **tiene** razón* (I think he's right). You are presenting it as a fact from your viewpoint, so no subjunctive.

cards.front: en mi opinión / desde mi punto de vista / para mí
cards.back: in my opinion / from my point of view / for me (in my view)
cards.notes: Set phrases to frame an opinion: **en mi opinión**, **desde mi punto de vista**, **para mí**. They are followed by a normal indicative clause: *Para mí, esta película **es** la mejor*. Note *para mí* carries an accent on *mí*.

cards.front: Creo que tiene razón. / No creo que tenga razón.
cards.back: affirmative opinion → indicative; negated opinion → subjunctive
cards.notes: The key flip for English speakers: an **affirmative** opinion asserts a fact → **indicative** (*Creo que **tiene** razón*). **Negate** the opinion verb and you introduce doubt → **subjunctive** (*No creo que **tenga** razón*). English keeps the same verb form both ways, so this switch is easy to forget.

cards.front: me parece bien/mal que · es lógico/normal que
cards.back: value judgments that trigger the subjunctive
cards.notes: Judging or evaluating an action triggers the **subjunctive** when the subject changes: **me parece bien/mal que**, **es lógico que**, **es normal que**, **es una pena que**. *Me parece mal que **llegues** tarde* (I think it's wrong that you arrive late).

cards.front: me molesta que · me sorprende que · me encanta que
cards.notes: Verbs of emotion take **que + subjunctive** when there are two subjects: **me molesta que**, **me sorprende que**, **me encanta que**, **es una pena que**. *Me molesta que **hablen** tan alto*. With no subject change, use the infinitive: *Me molesta **llegar** tarde*.

To agree: **estoy de acuerdo** and, crucially, **estar de acuerdo CON** someone — not *con* dropped or replaced. *Estoy de acuerdo **contigo***. Also **tienes razón** (you're right). Avoid translating 'agree with' word for word as *acordar con*.
To disagree politely: **no estoy de acuerdo** (con) and the very common **depende** (it depends). These let you push back without sounding harsh in a discussion.

cards.back: on the one hand... on the other (hand)...
cards.notes: To structure a balanced view: **por un lado... por otro (lado)...** *Por un lado es caro, por otro lado es muy cómodo.* Useful for nuanced opinions where you see both sides.

Certainty keeps the **indicative**: **estoy seguro de que** + indicative (*Estoy seguro de que **vendrá***). Note the **de** before *que*. As with opinions, certainty asserts a fact, so no subjunctive.

cards.front: no estoy seguro de que (+ subjunctive)
cards.notes: Negate the certainty and you get doubt → **subjunctive**: **no estoy seguro de que** + subjunctive (*No estoy seguro de que **venga***). Same affirmative-indicative / negated-subjunctive flip as with opinion verbs.

**Quizás** and **tal vez** (maybe) express possibility and typically take the **subjunctive** to stress uncertainty: *Quizás **llueva** mañana*. The indicative is possible if you feel more sure, but subjunctive is the safe, common choice.

cards.front: la alegría · la sorpresa · el enfado · la preocupación
cards.notes: Core feelings vocabulary for reactions: **la alegría** (joy), **la sorpresa** (surprise), **el enfado** (anger), **la preocupación** (worry). Pair them with the structures above: *Con gran **alegría**, me encanta que estés aquí*.

steps.title: Giving an opinion — and the mood flip
steps.body:
# Expressing opinions and feelings

This lesson is **functional language**: the phrases you need to say what you think, react to news, and agree or disagree.

## Giving an opinion

To state your view, use:

- **Creo que** / **Pienso que** / **Me parece que** + *indicative*
- **En mi opinión**, / **Desde mi punto de vista**, / **Para mí**, + *indicative*

> *Creo que esta idea **es** buena.*  ·  *Para mí, el español **no es** difícil.*

When you **assert** an opinion as true, Spanish uses the **indicative**. You are presenting a fact (from your perspective).

## The flip that trips up English speakers

Negate the opinion verb and the mood **changes to subjunctive**:

| Affirmative (assertion) → indicative | Negated (doubt) → subjunctive |
|---|---|
| Creo que **tiene** razón. | No creo que **tenga** razón. |
| Pienso que **viene**. | No pienso que **venga**. |
| Me parece que **es** verdad. | No me parece que **sea** verdad. |

In English the verb does not change (*I think he's right / I don't think he's right*), so it is easy to forget the switch. **Affirmative opinion = indicative; negated opinion = subjunctive.**

steps.title: Reacting: value judgments and emotions
steps.body:
## Value judgments + subjunctive

When you **evaluate** or **judge** an action (not just report it), use the **subjunctive** with a change of subject:

- **Me parece bien/mal que** + subjunctive
- **Es lógico que** · **Es normal que** · **Es una pena que** + subjunctive

> *Me parece mal que **llegues** tarde.*  ·  *Es una pena que no **puedan** venir.*

## Emotions + subjunctive

Verbs of **emotion and reaction** behave the same way:

- **Me molesta que** (it bothers me that)
- **Me sorprende que** (it surprises me that)
- **Me encanta que** (I love that)

> *Me encanta que **estés** aquí.*  ·  *Me molesta que **hablen** tan alto.*

**Two subjects → que + subjunctive.** If the subject does not change, use the **infinitive** instead:

- *Me molesta **llegar** tarde.* (I dislike arriving late — same subject)
- *Me molesta que tú **llegues** tarde.* (two subjects → subjunctive)

Feelings vocabulary to combine: **la alegría** (joy), **la sorpresa** (surprise), **el enfado** (anger), **la preocupación** (worry).

steps.title: Agreeing, disagreeing, and certainty vs doubt
steps.body:
## Agreeing and disagreeing

- **Estoy de acuerdo (con...)** — I agree (with...)
- **Tienes razón** — you're right
- **No estoy de acuerdo** — I disagree
- **Depende** — it depends
- **Por un lado... por otro (lado)...** — on the one hand... on the other...

> Watch out: it's **estar de acuerdo CON** alguien — *Estoy de acuerdo **contigo***. Do not drop *con*, and do not say *acordar con* for 'agree with'.

## Certainty vs doubt — the flip again

The same affirmative-indicative / negated-subjunctive pattern governs certainty:

| Certainty → indicative | Doubt → subjunctive |
|---|---|
| Estoy seguro de que **vendrá**. | No estoy seguro de que **venga**. |

Note the **de** in *estoy seguro **de** que*.

## Maybe: quizás / tal vez

**Quizás** and **tal vez** (maybe, perhaps) usually take the **subjunctive** to stress uncertainty:

> *Quizás **llueva** mañana.*  ·  *Tal vez **tengan** razón.*

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish expression with its English function.
Translate: *I don't think he's right.* (negated opinion → subjunctive)
exercise.hint: Negate the opinion verb → subjunctive; tener → tenga.

exercise.prompt: Fill in *tener* in the right mood. The opinion is affirmative, so use the indicative.
exercise.sentence: Creo que María ___ razón en este tema.
exercise.hint: Affirmative 'creo que' asserts a fact → indicative: tiene.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *It bothers me that you arrive late.* (emotion + change of subject → subjunctive)
accept: Me molesta que tú llegues tarde.
exercise.hint: me molesta que + subjunctive; llegar → llegues (tú).

exercise.prompt: Which sentence correctly says 'I'm not sure he'll come'? (doubt → subjunctive)

images.label: No estoy seguro de que venga.
No estoy seguro de que viene.
No estoy seguro de que venir.

exercise.hint: Doubt → subjunctive, and keep 'de que'; venir → venga.
distractors: No estoy seguro de que viene.
No estoy seguro de que venir.

steps.title: Build: I love that you're here

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *I love that you're here.* (emotion → subjunctive)
exercise.hint: me encanta que + subjunctive; estar → estés (tú).

exercise.prompt: Fill in *llover* after *quizás* to stress uncertainty (subjunctive).
exercise.sentence: Quizás ___ mañana, así que lleva el paraguas.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I agree with you.* (mind the preposition)
exercise.hint: estar de acuerdo CON; con + ti → contigo.

exercise.prompt: Match each feeling noun with its English meaning.
Translate: *It's a pity that they can't come.* (value judgment → subjunctive)
accept: Es una pena que no puedan venir.
es una pena que no puedan venir
Es una pena que no puedan venir
Es una pena que no puedan venir.
exercise.hint: Es una pena que + subjunctive; poder → puedan (ellos).
distractors: Es una pena que no pueden venir.
Es una pena que no poder venir.
Es una pena que no podrán venir.

exercise.prompt: Fill in *ser* in the right mood after a negated opinion.
exercise.sentence: No me parece que esta ___ la mejor solución.
exercise.hint: 'No me parece que' negates the opinion → subjunctive; ser → sea.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I'm sure (that) she'll come tomorrow.* (certainty → indicative)
accept: Estoy seguro de que vendrá mañana.
estoy seguro de que vendra manana
Estoy seguro de que vendrá mañana
Estoy segura de que vendrá mañana.
exercise.hint: estoy seguro DE que + indicative; venir → vendrá (future).
distractors: Estoy seguro de que venga mañana.
Estoy seguro de que venir mañana.
